Jae Woong Hwang Resume Experience Toolchains

Technical Case Study

Turning a Legacy Calibration Setup into an ADAS AI Validation Loop

A resource-constrained real-vehicle AI validation prototype built from the commercial-vehicle ADAS team’s existing CANape/VN1640 laptop workflow.

PythonC++CANape/VN1640XCP over CAN-FDONNXAI Validation Transition
Back to resume
ContextProblemConstraintDecisionImpactImplementationBoundary
01

Context

Commercial-vehicle ADAS teams need to prepare for AI-based validation while still supporting production programs, calibration work, and field-test schedules. In this environment, the practical workflow was built around CANape, VN1640, a laptop, XCP/CAN-FD, flashing, calibration, and measurement.

Unlike better-equipped AI/HPC validation environments, this workflow did not assume immediate access to Ethernet-based interface hardware or Thor/Orin-class compute platforms. The project focused on what could be built using the equipment already available to the team.

02

Problem

ADAS module owners needed a way to connect learned-model concepts to real vehicle signals without waiting for a full future AI validation infrastructure. The challenge was not only communication bandwidth; it was helping engineers move from conventional calibration practice into a repeatable AI validation loop.

03

Constraint

  • Field workflow centered on CANape, VN1640, and laptop-based measurement/calibration.
  • Ethernet validation hardware and high-end AI compute platforms were not readily available in the commercial-vehicle team environment.
  • The prototype had to remain understandable to ADAS module owners already familiar with calibration workflows.
  • Public portfolio material had to avoid proprietary ECU signals, CAN database details, calibration values, and real logs.
04

Decision

I chose not to wait for ideal infrastructure. I reused the existing calibration setup and built a minimum viable bypass loop on top of XCP over CAN-FD, with a C++ vehicle-facing bridge and a Python runtime layer for visualization and learned-model experimentation.

The engineering point was not that XCP/CAN-FD is the ideal AI transport. The value was converting a legacy calibration setup into a practical stepping stone toward future Ethernet/HPC-based validation.

05

Impact

AI transitionGave module owners a practical way to understand learned-model validation using familiar tools.
Resource constraintAvoided dependence on unavailable Ethernet/HPC infrastructure for early learning and experimentation.
Vehicle relevanceConnected model experiments to real-vehicle signal workflows rather than only offline notebooks.
06

Implementation

  • XCP-over-CAN-FD access path through the available calibration setup.
  • C++ backend for vehicle-facing data movement and timing-sensitive communication.
  • Python UI/runtime layer for signal mapping, model execution, and operator-facing review.
  • ONNX/Python model loading path for learned-model experimentation.
  • AI-assisted coding was used for implementation acceleration, while architecture, requirements, testing, debugging, and engineering decisions remained human-owned.
Production ECU / vehicle signals ↓ CANape + VN1640 legacy field workflow ↓ C++ communication bridge ↓ Python runtime + ONNX model experiment ↓ Validation review by module owner
07

Public-Safe Boundary

This page contains a public-safe technical summary only. Proprietary source code, ECU signal names, CAN database content, calibration metadata, thresholds, vehicle logs, internal screenshots, and company-confidential implementation details are intentionally omitted.
